modelAnswer:
MotoCorp is a manufacturing firm where managers emphasize authority, close supervision, and strict discipline. Employees enjoy job security but report low motivation and lack of creativity. Based on the scenario, answer the following questions:
i. Identify and define the OB model MotoCorp is following. (1 mark)
ii. What are the advantages and disadvantages of this model? (1 mark)
iii. Suggest a shift to a more suitable OB model and justify your choice. (1 mark)
i. Identify and define the OB model MotoCorp is following. (1 mark)
MotoCorp is following the Custodial Model of Organizational Behaviour.
This model is based on the economic resources of the organization, where
employees' needs for security and welfare are fulfilled. Managers focus on
providing job security and benefits, which results in employees'
dependence on the organization rather than on managerial authority alone.
ii. What are the advantages and disadvantages of this model? (1 mark)
Advantages:
Provides job security and stable employment.
Creates a sense of loyalty and dependence on the organization.
Reduces resistance to authority, as employees feel secure.
Disadvantages:
Employees may feel passive and dependent, leading to low motivation and
creativity.
Lack of challenge and empowerment can reduce innovation and initiative.
Productivity may remain at a mediocre level.
iii. Suggest a shift to a more suitable OB model and justify your choice.
(1 mark)
MotoCorp should shift to the Supportive Model.
This model emphasizes leadership, motivation, and participation, rather
than just authority or security.
It encourages employee involvement, creativity, and job satisfaction.
By empowering employees and valuing their contributions, the organization
can move from passive cooperation to active engagement and higher
productivity.

modelAnswer:
Briefly explain the three components of attitude?
Briefly explain the three components of attitude?
Attitude is a learned predisposition to respond positively or negatively toward a person, object, or situation. It has three components:
Cognitive Component: Refers to the beliefs, thoughts, or knowledge a person has about something.
Example: "Working in a team increases productivity."
Affective Component: Refers to the emotions or feelings a person has toward something.
Example: Feeling happy and motivated when assigned a challenging project.
Behavioural Component: Refers to the intent to act or observable actions based on beliefs and feelings.

Imagine you are a manager of a manufacturing team. Half your employees constantly look for guidance, while the other half show initiative and creativity.

Questions:
i. If you apply only Theory X to the whole team, what will happen? (1 mark)
ii. If you apply only Theory Y, what risks might occur? (1 mark)
iii. As manager, how will you balance your leadership style between
Suggest four organizational practices that can help employees with low self-esteem gain confidence.
Create a personality profile of an ideal IT Project Manager by combining the Big Five Personality traits, Locus of Control, and key dimensions of Emotional Intelligence (EI).

Identify and explain two strengths that may support Trisha's success as a project manager. (1 mark)
If you apply only Theory X to the whole team, what will happen? (1 mark)
Theory X assumes employees are lazy, need constant supervision, and avoid responsibility. Applying it to the entire team would demotivate the self-motivated, creative employees, stifle initiative, and reduce overall productivity.
Identify and explain two strengths that may support Trisha's success as a project manager. (1 mark)

Strong Locus of Control (Internal): Trisha believes that her effort and planning determine project outcomes. This means she is proactive, responsible, and persistent, which is essential for meeting project goals.

Self-Awareness and Self-Regulation (Emotional Intelligence): She understands her own emotions and maintains composure under pressure, helping her make thoughtful decisions and manage stressful situations effectively.

Identify and explain two weaknesses that might hinder her success. (1 mark)
If you apply only Theory Y, what risks might occur? (1 mark)
Theory Y assumes employees are self-motivated, responsible, and enjoy work. Applying it to the dependent employees who require guidance may lead to confusion, mistakes, and lack of discipline, as they may struggle without clear supervision.
Identify and explain two weaknesses that might hinder her success. (1 mark)

Low Self-Esteem in Leadership: Doubting her leadership abilities and second-guessing decisions, especially under senior management scrutiny, may reduce her confidence and decisiveness, negatively affecting team trust.

Difficulty in Team Networking and Conflict Management: Struggling to motivate large groups and handle conflicts can lead to team dissatisfaction, micromanagement perception, and project delays, particularly with diverse personality types on the team.

Based on these strengths and weaknesses, evaluate whether Trisha is likely to succeed in the long run. Support your answer with three arguments linked to the above concepts. (2 marks)
As manager, how will you balance your leadership style between Theory X and Y? (1 mark)
Use an adaptive approach:
Apply Theory X management (close supervision, clear instructions) to employees who need guidance.
Apply Theory Y management (delegation, empowerment, encouragement of creativity) to self-motivated employees.
This approach maximizes productivity while maintaining motivation and creativity across the team.
Based on these strengths and weaknesses, evaluate whether Trisha is likely to succeed in the long run. Support your answer with three arguments linked to the above concepts. (2 marks)

Proactive and Structured Approach: Her internal locus of control and preference for structured work means she takes ownership and plans effectively, which is a strong foundation for project management success.

Need for Leadership Confidence Development: Low self-esteem may limit her growth unless she builds confidence through mentorship, feedback, and small leadership wins, enabling her to make decisive and assertive choices.

Importance of Enhancing Emotional Intelligence in Team Management:
To succeed long-term, Trisha must improve social skills and conflict management, leveraging her self-awareness to adapt leadership style to different team members' needs.

Verdict: Trisha has the potential to succeed due to her planning, responsibility, and self-regulation.
Success will depend on her developing confidence and interpersonal skills to manage team dynamics and gain trust.
Imagine you are a manager of a manufacturing team. Half your employees constantly look for guidance, while the other half show initiative and creativity.

Suggest four organizational practices that can help employees with low self-esteem gain confidence.
Training and Skill Development: Provide workshops, on-the-job training, and mentorship programs to help employees gain competence and feel capable.
Positive Feedback and Recognition: Regularly acknowledge achievements, effort, and improvement to reinforce self-worth.
Assigning Challenging but Achievable Tasks: Gradually increasing responsibility helps employees experience success and build confidence.
Encouraging Participation in Decision-Making: Involve employees in team discussions and decisions to make them feel valued and capable.
Mentoring and Coaching: Pair employees with experienced mentors who can guide, encourage, and provide constructive feedback.
Creating a Supportive Work Environment: Promote open communication, collaboration, and psychological safety, so employees feel comfortable taking initiatives without fear of criticism.
Celebrating Small Wins: Recognizing incremental progress helps employees see their growth and reinforces self-confidence.

Create a personality profile of an ideal IT Project Manager by combining the Big Five Personality traits, Locus of Control, and key dimensions of Emotional Intelligence (EI).
Personality Profile of an Ideal IT Project Manager

Big Five Personality Traits
Openness to Experience: High
Willing to explore innovative solutions, adapt to new technologies, and
embrace creative problem-solving.
Conscientiousness: Very High
Organized, dependable, detail-oriented, and capable of delivering projects
on time.
Extraversion: Moderate to High
Effective in communication, motivating team members, and stakeholder
management without being overly dominant.
Agreeableness: High
Cooperative, supportive, and able to maintain positive team relationships
while resolving conflicts diplomatically.
Neuroticism (Emotional Stability): Low
Calm under pressure, resilient to stress, and able to make rational
decisions in challenging situations.

Locus of Control
Internal Locus of Control: High
Believes outcomes depend on their own actions, takes responsibility for
project success, and proactively addresses challenges.

Key Dimensions of Emotional Intelligence (EI)
Self-Awareness: Recognizes own strengths, weaknesses, and emotions
to manage behavior effectively.
Self-Regulation: Maintains composure, handles stress, and responds
thoughtfully rather than reactively.
Motivation: Intrinsically driven to achieve goals, inspire the team, and
pursue continuous improvement.
Empathy: Understands team members' perspectives and needs,
facilitating better communication and conflict resolution.
Social Skills: Builds strong relationships, influences stakeholders,
negotiates effectively, and fosters teamwork.
